topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The vibrant, overly cheerful bouquet was dropped onto my worn kitchen counter with a soft thud. I stared at the arrangement—a riot of synthetic pinks and aggressively yellow lilies—and felt a tightening in my chest, a familiar knot of resentment. No note. Just flowers. It was a gesture so saccharine, so utterly misplaced, that it made my jaw clench. I picked up a stem, the petals feeling slick and artificial under my fingertips, and deliberately crushed a few of the delicate edges. "What is this supposed to be?" I muttered to the empty room, my voice sharp and brittle. I shoved the bunch toward the doorway, not wanting to look at it again, already formulating a plan to discard the unwelcome intrusion.

[story 2]

I found the package tucked beneath my apartment door, the wrapping paper crinkling loudly as I tore it open. Inside, the flowers were undeniably beautiful, a sickening display of forced sweetness. A wave of pure irritation washed over me, making my skin prickle with a cold, defensive energy. I didn't even bother looking for a card; the absence of explanation was, in itself, a statement. I tossed the stems onto the floor, the blooms scattering like fallen confetti. I stood there, breathing deeply, trying to steady the tremor in my hands. This unsolicited display of affection felt like an imposition, a needless burden. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights, wishing the whole ridiculous thing would just vanish.

[story 3]

When the delivery person handed me the arrangement, their smile seemed too wide, too practiced. The flowers were bright, almost offensively so, demanding attention. I took them, my fingers brushing against the cool, smooth stems, and felt a surge of profound annoyance. I didn't offer thanks; instead, I held the bunch stiffly, my shoulders rising toward my ears in a posture of stiff resistance. I turned away from the entrance, my gaze fixed on the floor tiles, refusing to acknowledge the gesture. Every vibrant color seemed to mock the dull, heavy atmosphere I carried inside. I tucked the flowers haphazardly into a vase in the corner, a silent, tangible reminder of something I actively wished to ignore.
